coredata.dc:title: Blue-red self-calibrating signals for aqueous oxygen from a composite structure of carbon dots and Ru probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaojuan
Ye, Yun
Ye, Xiuya
Li, Ruimin
Liao, Yongqiang
coredata.dc:description:
The detection of dissolved oxygen in aqueous solutions is an important issue in various fields. In this work, we prepared efficient blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs, emission wavelength=453 nm, quantum yield=25.8%)) using the fibers of Euonymus bungeanus as a starting material through a classic hydrothermal method, serving as the reference, and red-emitting Ru(II) compounds (emission wavelength=705-710 nm, quantum yield=9.2-10.4%), which harvested the blue emission and were thus excited by the CDs, serving as the probe. Ru(II) complexes with terpyridine-derived ligands having different bonding groups (-NH2 and -COOH) were compared to find the optimal sensing performance. The covalent bonding between the carbon dots and the Ru(II) probe was expected to exhibit ratiometric sensing signals for dissolved oxygen. The Ru(II) probes and the CDs were discussed carefully, including single molecular structure, electronic transition, absorption, emission, excitation, quantum yields, and lifetime, which confirmed an energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe. The resulting composite sample (denoted as Ru1@CDs) was analyzed by micromorphology analysis, XRD, IR, and elemental analysis. A probe loading level was determined as 18.45%, with an energy transfer efficiency (CDs → probe) as high as 26.3%. Ru1@CDs showed good sensing selectivity and a linear correlation with the concentration of dissolved oxygen, with a fitting equation of
I 0
/
I
= 1.462 + 3.716 · [
O 2
]
, a LOD of 0.12 mg/L, and a short response time of ∼140 s. The sensing mechanism was proven as a dynamic collision between the Ru(II) probe and O2. The novelty of this work was the combination of two luminescent components and the corresponding emission color change from deep-red at low [O2] levels (∼0.6 mg/L) to blue at high [O2] levels (∼10 mg/L).
coredata.dcterms:subject: dissolved oxygen
optical sensing
composite structure
ratiometric signal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of dissolved molecular oxygen by Ru1@CD and Ru2@CD
Characterization on Ru1, Ru2, and CDs
Electronic transitions and simulated absorption spectra of Ru1 and Ru2
Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs
Emission quantum yields and lifetimes of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs
Loading level and status via IR spectra
Emission, excitation, excited state lifetime, and FRET of Ru1@CD and Ru2@CDs
Signal stability, response time and practical performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular structure of Ru1 and Ru2, and the synthetic strategy of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs.
Geometric structure of Ru1, Ru2, and their inter-molecular stacking. See Supporting Information for their detailed geometric parameters.
Frontier molecular orbitals of Ru1 (a: HOMO, b: LUMO), Ru2 (c: HOMO, d: LUMO), and their simulated absorption spectra (e). Calculated at RB3LYP/LANL2DZ level.
Absorption spectra (a), excitation spectra (b), emission spectra (c), and excited state lifetimes (d) of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs dispersed in degassed double-distilled water (10 mg/mL).
XRD patterns (a) of CDs, Ru1@CDs, and Ru2@CDs. TEM images of CDs (b), Ru1@CDs (c), and Ru2@CDs (d). Magnified TEM images of CDs (b insets), Ru1@CDs (c inset), and Ru2@CDs (d inset).
IR spectra (a) of CDs, Ru1, Ru1@CDs, and Ru2@CDs. Emission (b) spectra of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs. Excitation spectra (c) and excited state lifetimes (d) of Ru1@CDs (λem=701 nm) and Ru2@CDs (λem=695 nm).

Emission spectra (a) of Ru1@CDs upon various [O2] levels. Inset a: a photo of Ru1@CDs plate with three aqueous drops of various [O2] levels (from lower left corner to higher right corner: ∼0.6 mg/L, ∼5 mg/L, and ∼10 mg/L).
I 0
/
I
vs [O2] and
I 459
/
I 701
vs [O2] plots and their fitting lines (b). Inset b: intensity values at 459 nm and 701 nm upon various [O2] levels. Emission spectra (c) of Ru1@CDs upon common competing species and interferents (50 μM), including blank (0, [O2]<1 mg/L]), Ca2+ (1), Mg2+ (2), Fe2+ (3), Fe3+ (4), Co2+ (5), Ni2+ (6), Cu2+ (7), Zn2+ (8), Hg2+ (9), H+ (10), H2O2 (11), GSH (12), and O2 (13, [O2]=∼40 mg/L]). Emission monitoring (d) at 701 nm with [O2] increased.
Photophysical parameters of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a, MLCT peaks of Ru1 and Ru2 were listed, CDs showed broad absorption and there are multiple absorption peaks at wavelength shorter than 230 nm.
b, wide excitation window was observed.
c, biexponential decay, the weighted mean average value was calculated by (A1τ1 2+A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1+A2τ2)

a, the concentrations of dissolved oxygen were determined by the fitted equations and the standard method (Winkler). The reference concentrations (by Winkler method) were determined as 1, 3.14 mg/L; 2, 9.88 mg/L; 3, 38.47 mg/L. Tap water was firstly boiled and then bubbled with N2 stream for 3 min. to eliminate dissolved O2, and then bubbled with O2 stream. The values in “()” were recovery values.

head.ce:title: Blue-red self-calibrating signals for aqueous oxygen from a composite structure of carbon dots and Ru probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Guangdong Industry Polytechnic University, Guangzhou 510300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecological Environment Technology, Guangdong Industry Polytechnic University, Guangzhou 510300, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2College of Ecological Environment Technology, Guangdong Industry Polytechnic University, Guangzhou 510300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Silv Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, Guangzhou 510507, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Silv Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 3Guangdong Silv Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, Guangzhou 510507, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The detection of dissolved oxygen in aqueous solutions is an important issue in various fields. In this work, we prepared efficient blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs, emission wavelength=453 nm, quantum yield=25.8%)) using the fibers of Euonymus bungeanus as a starting material through a classic hydrothermal method, serving as the reference, and red-emitting Ru(II) compounds (emission wavelength=705-710 nm, quantum yield=9.2-10.4%), which harvested the blue emission and were thus excited by the CDs, serving as the probe. Ru(II) complexes with terpyridine-derived ligands having different bonding groups (-NH2 and -COOH) were compared to find the optimal sensing performance. The covalent bonding between the carbon dots and the Ru(II) probe was expected to exhibit ratiometric sensing signals for dissolved oxygen. The Ru(II) probes and the CDs were discussed carefully, including single molecular structure, electronic transition, absorption, emission, excitation, quantum yields, and lifetime, which confirmed an energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe. The resulting composite sample (denoted as Ru1@CDs) was analyzed by micromorphology analysis, XRD, IR, and elemental analysis. A probe loading level was determined as 18.45%, with an energy transfer efficiency (CDs → probe) as high as 26.3%. Ru1@CDs showed good sensing selectivity and a linear correlation with the concentration of dissolved oxygen, with a fitting equation of
I 0
/
I
= 1.462 + 3.716 · [
O 2
]
, a LOD of 0.12 mg/L, and a short response time of ∼140 s. The sensing mechanism was proven as a dynamic collision between the Ru(II) probe and O2. The novelty of this work was the combination of two luminescent components and the corresponding emission color change from deep-red at low [O2] levels (∼0.6 mg/L) to blue at high [O2] levels (∼10 mg/L).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a class of novel carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) have shown advantages of facile source, easy synthesis, low cost, adjustable and stable properties, which endows CDs with various applications as a luminescent material, such as optical sensing, imaging, and smart delivery/release [1,2]. To meet practical requirements, carbon dots with superior properties have been synthesized by “top-down” and “bottom up” methods, where the former includes laser irradiation, ablation treatment, arc-discharge, and electrochemical exfoliation, and the latter synthesizes carbon dots by hydrothermal and microwave techniques using light molecules as the starting reagent [3–5]. The corresponding carbon dots have been widely used in aqueous-based optical sensing platforms due to their attractive optical performance. For example, a CD-based nanosensing platform for aqueous Fe3+ detection was reported by Zhu, which was synthesized by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as starting reagents [6].

Being an important analyte in the fields of biological research and medical analysis, dissolved oxygen is usually mentioned. Some efforts have been devoted to the exploration of optical sensing platforms for aqueous dissolved oxygen. For example, an emission turn-on sensing platform for dissolved oxygen was constructed by Sun and coworkers using CDs as a sensing probe [7]. By employing two emissive centers, CDs and Ru(II), Wang and coworkers synthesized a ratiometric sensing platform for dissolved oxygen with emission bands from CDs and Ru(II) [8]. Although ratiometric sensing has been proved to be an improved pathway to improve sensing selectivity, the practical ratiometric sensing platforms are still insufficient. The ratiometric sensing utilizes the ratio of two emission intensities as the sensing output, one of which should be oxygen-sensitive, serving as the probe, while the other should be oxygen-insensitive, serving as a reference, so that self-calibrated sensing signals can be generated [9]. However, the narrow excitation/emission bands of the CDs make it difficult to find a suitable probe (or reference) to generate dual emission bands for ratiometric sensing.

Literatures have suggested Ru(II) complexes as an efficient oxygen-sensitive probe, due to their MLCT-based long-lived emission (MLCT=metal-to-ligand-charge-transfer) [8,10]. Their MLCT transitions (optimal excitation wavelength) generally localize in blue region around 450 nm. To construct a ratiometric sensing platform, carbon dots with efficient blue emission (around 450 nm) should be applied, so that efficient energy transfer (ET) from the carbon dots to the Ru(II) probe can be realized. Guided by the above consideration, in this work, we prepared efficient blue-emitting carbon dots, which were synthesized by a classic hydrothermal method, serving as the reference, and red-emitting Ru(II) compounds, which harvested the blue emission and were thus excited by the CDs, serving as the probe. The fibers of Euonymus bungeanus, which were economic, long, and regular fibers, were used as the starting material because the resulting CDs exhibited a suitable emission wavelength that overlapped well with the optimal excitation window of the Ru(II) compounds, which ensured an efficient energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe and allowed for further applications [11]. Ru(II) complexes with terpyridine-derived ligands having different bonding groups (-NH2 and -COOH) were compared to find the optimal sensing performance. The combination of the carbon dots and the Ru(II) probe was supposed to exhibit ratiometric sensing signals for dissolved oxygen.

The molecular structure of the two Ru(II) compounds (denoted as Ru1 and Ru2) and the preparation strategy of the sensing platforms (denoted as Ru1@CD and Ru2@CD) are shown in Scheme 1 . All synthesis-related chemical reagents (analytical reagent grade) were commercially obtained and used as received, including N-ethylmorpholine, RuCl3•nH2O (CAS 10049-08-8), 2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine (CAS 1148-79-4), [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid (CAS 148332-36-9), [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridin]-4′-amine (CAS 193944-66-0), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (shortened as EDC, CAS 25952-53-8), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (shortened as NHS, CAS 6066-82-6). The fibers of Euonymus bungeanus were obtained from our university and dried before use. NMR result was determined using a Varian INOVA 300 spectrometer. Crysal diffraction data were recorded using a Bruker D4 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation (λ=1.5405 Å, voltage=40 kV, current=30 mA). Elemental analysis was performed using a Carlo Erba 1106 elemental analyzer. Theoretical calculations were performed on Ru1 and Ru2 with the TD-DFT (time-dependent density functional theory) method at the RB3LYP/LANL2DZ level in a vacuum environment using Orca (version 5.04) and Firefly (version 8.2). The initial geometry was constructed using their single crystal structures. The selected frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) were plotted using wxMcMolPlt (version 7.7.3, contour value=0.025). The simulated absorption spectra were plotted using Multiwfn (version 3.8) with the first 50 singlet electronic transitions calculated by Orca, as aforementioned. The photoluminescence (PL) and absorption result was determined using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (luminescence mode, slits=2 nm×2 nm) and a Shimadzu UV-3101 PC spectrophotometer, respectively. The lifetimes of Ru(II) excited states were determined using an FL920 spectrometer. The emission quantum yields (QYs = ϕ) were determined following a literature method, using quinine sulfate (in 1.0 M sulfuric acid, QY = 0.546) as a reference [12]. The CIE coordinates were provided by a SpectraScan PR650 spectrometer. The micromorphology (SEM and TEM) images were shot using a Hitachi S-4800 microscope and a JEOL JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope, respectively.

Ru1 and Ru2 were synthesized following literature procedures [13–15]. First, Ru(2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl3 and Ru([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridin]-4′-amine)Cl3 were prepared following the procedure below. To a 250 mL round-bottom flask, 120 mL of dehydrated ethanol, 10 mmol of RuCl3•nH2O, and 10 mmol of 2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine (or [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridin]-4′-amine) were added and mixed together. The mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 4 h and then cooled to room temperature. The reddish yellow solution was filtered to give brown powder. The crude product was washed with ethanol and Et2O three times and then dried at ambient conditions (yield: ∼82%). 5 mmol of the above-obtained Ru(2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl3 (or Ru([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridin]-4′-amine)Cl3) and 5 mmol of [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid were mixed with 40 mL of ethanol and 10 mL of H2O, then 1 mL of N-ethylmorpholine was added. The resulting mixture was stirred under N2 atmosphere at 85 °C for 4 h and then cooled to room temperature. The resulting solid product was filtered and washed ethanol and Et2O for three times and then dried at ambient condition.

With the fibers of Euonymus bungeanus as a starting material, the blue-emitting carbon dots (denoted as CDs) were prepared following a literature procedure [16]. The pre-dried and cleaned fibers of Euonymus bungeanus (1.5 g) were crushed by a ball crusher (@1200 rpm for 10 min). The resulting powder (0.7 g) was dispersed into a mixture of ethylenediamine (1.02 g) and double-distilled water (20 mL). After being treated with an ultrasonic bath for 30 min, the resulting suspension was sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave (capacity=30 mL) and heated at 200°C for 10 h (see Table S1 and Table S2 of Supporting Information for the optimization of reaction conditions). After cooling, the resulting mixture was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) against 500 mL of double-distilled water. The dialysis procedure was repeated for another two times. The crude product was freeze-dried and collected as a brown powder (yield: 0.10 g), dispersed with double-distilled water (10 mg/mL), and stored at 4°C for later use.

Ru1 (or Ru2, 0.3 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of double-distilled water and treated with an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. Later, dilute HCl was added dropwise to adjust the pH of this solution to ∼6. After adding EDC (0.75 g) and NHS (0.45 g), the resulting mixture was allowed to react at ambient condition for 2 h under vigorous stirring, then 30 mL of the aforementioned CDs suspension (10 mg/mL) was added dropwise. The resulting suspension was allowed to react at ambient conditions for 12 h under vigorous stirring. The final suspension was placed in a centrifuge for 10 minutes (5500×g). The upper transparent layer was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) against 500 mL of double-distilled water. The dialysis procedure was repeated for another four times until the solution became non-fluorescent. The crude product was freeze-dried and collected as a brown powder (yield: 0.08 g), dispersed with double-distilled water (10 mg/mL, bubbled with a pure N2 stream prior to use to eliminate dissolved oxygen), and stored at 4°C for later use. See Figure S1 (Supporting Information) for their mass spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of dissolved molecular oxygen by Ru1@CD and Ru2@CD
ce:section.ce:para: Oxygen-free water was prepared prior to use by double-distilled water. A pure N2 stream was bubbled for 15 min to remove the dissolved oxygen. The pure N2 and O2 streams were adjusted using gas meters and mixed together at desired ratios, and then bubbled into the solutions to adjust the concentration of dissolved oxygen. Each solution was equally divided into two parts and sealed in two Winkler bottles. One Winkler bottle was analyzed by a standard procedure for determining dissolved oxygen (GB7489-87, China), and the other one was mixed with the above-obtained Run@CD suspension to determine the concentration of dissolved oxygen by the optical sensing method. The response time was measured using the Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer under “Time Scan” mode with a monitoring wavelength of 701 nm. The emission intensity was continuously monitored for a suspension with a low level of dissolved oxygen (<1 mg/L), with another suspension with a high level of dissolved oxygen (∼40 mg/L) quickly added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization on Ru1, Ru2, and CDs

ce:section.ce:para: It has been aforementioned in Section 2.3 that the CDs were prepared following a hydrothermal method using ethylenediamine as a stabilizer, which resulted in the surface of the resulting carbon dots being full of -NH2 groups. To ensure solid bonding between the carbon dots and the Ru probe, a -COOH group was incorporated into the Ru(II) probe to form a covalent bond, hoping to avoid phase separation between the CDs and the Ru(II) probe. There is only one -COOH in the Ru1 molecule. On the other hand, Ru2 has both a -COOH group and a -NH2 group, and this -NH2 group allows further bonding with another Ru2 molecule, which increases the probe loading ratio in the composite structure (Ru@CD) and offers stronger probe emission intensity (signal). Fig. 1 shows the molecular structures of [Ru1]Cl2 and [Ru2]Cl2. Each Ru(II) ion is coordinated by two terpyridine (or terpyridine-derived) ligands, forming an octahedral field, which is consistent with literature reports (see the Supporting Information for the detailed geometric structures) [13–15]. The Cl ions are not attached to the Ru(II) ion and are far away from it, meaning that they act only as counterions. There is only one molecule in each Ru1 unit cell, whereas two molecules are found in each Ru2 unit cell. Due to the large coplanar conjugation planes of terpyridine and terpyridine-derived ligands in Ru1 and Ru2, these planes are orderly aligned, with a face-to-face distance of 3.277 Å in Ru1 and 3.561 Å in Ru2, respectively, indicating a π-π interaction between these planes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic transitions and simulated absorption spectra of Ru1 and Ru2
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported, typical [Ru(N-N)3]2+ compounds exhibit MLCT-based transitions during photo-induced activities such as absorption and emission, where MLCT stands for metal-to-ligand-charge-transfer [10,17,18]. These long-lived excited sates collide with O2 molecules and transfer their energy to O2 molecules, which is known as a dynamic mechanism, leading to sensing responses to O2 such as emission quenching and diminished excited state lifetime [10,17,18]. To gain a quick understanding of the electronic transitions in Ru1 and Ru2, their selected frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs), HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital), are analyzed by the TD-DFT (time-dependent density functional theory) method at the RB3LYP/LANL2DZ level in a vacuum environment. As shown in Fig. 2 , the occupied FMOs of Ru1 and Ru2 consist of the d orbitals of the Ru(II) ion, admixed with contributions from the terpyridine-based ligands. As for the LUMOs of Ru1 and Ru2, they are mainly composed of the d orbitals of Ru(II) ion and the π* of the terpyridine-based ligands. It is observed that the electron donor (the -NH2 group) of [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridin]-4′-amine increases its contribution to the HOMO in Ru2, but contributes little to the LUMO in Ru2. With this result on hand, it is concluded that the electronic transitions between the HOMOs and the LUMOs of Ru1 and Ru2 shall have a dominant character of MLCT, admixed with d-d transition character of Ru(II). It is well-known that the d-d transitions are prohibited by the law of spin selection [10,17,18]. Thus, the over-participation of d-d transitions in the FMOs (mainly the LUMO) tends to compromise the PL performance via a non-radiative decay pathway, leading to low emission intensity and a short excited state lifetime. As for a probe for oxygen sensing, a long lifetime of excited state is desired since it gives more chances of dynamic collision between the probe and the O2 molecule and thus improves sensing performance. Thus, it is tentatively assumed that the electron donor (the -NH2 group) in Ru2 is beneficial for PL (photoluminescence) and sensing performance, as the participation of Ru d orbitals in the LUMO is reduced by the participation of the -NH2 group. This assumption will be further explained and validated (or disproved) by the experimental result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selected photophysical parameters of Ru1, Ru2, and the CDs are summarized in Table 1 , including absorption peaks (λabs), excitation peaks (λex), emission peaks (λem), FWHM (full-width-at-half-maximum) values, and emission quantum yields (ϕ). The detailed absorption spectra of Ru1, Ru2, and the CDs are shown in Fig. 3 . Due to their similar molecular structures, the absorption spectra of Ru1 and Ru2 are comparable, showing MLCT-based bands at 430 nm for Ru1 and 415 nm for Ru2, and ligand π-π*-based bands at ∼240 nm, respectively [10,17,18]. These absorption bands are consistent with the simulated absorption bands shown in Fig. 2. The electron donor (the -NH2 group) in Ru2 causes the MLCT-based band to red shift by 15 nm, along with a slightly increased absorbance of Ru2 compared to that of Ru1, which aligns with the simulated absorption spectra shown in Fig. 2. The -NH2 group lifts the π* energy level of [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridin]-4′-amine which is basically the LUMO of Ru2, while the HOMO energy level of Ru2 generally remains due to the participation of Ru(II) d orbitals. Therefore, the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO of Ru2 is larger than that of Ru1. For CDs, a wide and broad absorption spectrum is observed, with no vibronic progressions. This result indicates that the CDs is an efficient photon-harvesting system.

The excitation spectra of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs are shown in Fig. 3. The two Ru(II) compounds exhibit a wide excitation window ranging from 234 nm to 451 nm for Ru1 and 234 nm to 433 nm for Ru2, respectively. Although the absorbance of the MLCT-based transitions (wavelength>400 nm) is weaker than that of π-π*-based transitions (wavelength<350 nm), their excitation efficiencies are comparable. The different energy transfer paths of MLCT-based and π-π*-based transitions are likely responsible. Since the emissive state originates from the MLCT excited state, the former (MLCT-based) transition can directly transfer its energy to the emissive center without undergoing further energy-exhausting procedures, resulting in high excitation efficiency [19]. The latter (π-π*-based) transition, however, must undergo a series of energy-exhausting procedures before transferring its energy to the emissive center, such as internal conversions and intersystem crossing, resulting in decreased excitation efficiency. The CDs, with a wide and broad absorption spectrum and no vibronic progressions, exhibit two excitation peaks at 248 nm and 353 nm. The observation of a definite excitation band means an energy gap between conduction and valence bands. The radiative decay between conduction and valence bands is supposed to give emission.

Fig. 3 shows the emission spectra of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs. Ru1 and Ru2 exhibit single emission bands, peaking at 710 nm for Ru1 and 705 nm for Ru2, respectively. Due to their charge-transfer-based excited states, no vibronic progressions are observed for their emission bands, with FWHM values of ∼66 nm. Compared to Ru(II) complexes with bipyridine-derived ligands whose emission wavelength is approximately 620 nm, Ru1 and Ru2 exhibit a red shift in their emission, peaking at 710 nm for Ru1 and 705 nm for Ru2, respectively [10,17,18]. This red shift in emission for Ru1 and Ru2 is attributed to their terpyridine-derived ligands, which have a larger conjugation size compared to bipyridine-derived ligands. The emission of Ru2 is slightly blue-shifted compared to that of Ru1, due to the -NH2 group which elevates the LUMO of Ru2, as mentioned earlier. The CDs exhibit blue emission peaking at 453 nm with an FWHM of 63 nm, and this emission band overlaps well with the optimal excitation window of Ru1 and Ru2, indicating a possible energy transfer from the CDs to Ru1/Ru2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission quantum yields and lifetimes of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission quantum yields (ϕ) and lifetimes (τ) of Ru1, Ru2, and CDs are recorded and listed in Table 1. The CDs exhibit a blue emission band with a quantum yield as high as 25.8%, and this efficient emission makes the CDs an energy donor for the excitation of Ru1 and Ru2, as will be further confirmed and discussed. The ϕ values of Ru1 (ϕ=10.4%) and Ru2 (ϕ=9.2%) are relatively low compared to superior luminescent dyes (ϕ>50%), which is attributed to the combination of the spin-forbidden d-d transitions (non-emissive) with the MLCT excited state (emissive) [10,17]. The vibration of the -NH2 group slightly quenches Ru2’s emission, resulting in a slightly lower ϕ value (9.2%) compared to that of Ru1 (ϕ=10.4%).

The quenching effect of the -NH2 group becomes evident when comparing the lifetimes of Ru1 and Ru2 (see Figure S2, Figure S3, and Figure S4 of Supporting Information for detailed fitting parameters). As shown in Table 1, the lifetime of Ru2 (τ=139.8 ns) is significantly shorter than that of Ru1 (τ=168.8 ns), owing to the thermal vibration of the active -NH2 group in Ru2. These two lifetime values are significantly shorter than those of typical MLCT-based emissions, such as phosphorescent Cu(I) complexes (τ∼μs) and Re(I) complexes (τ∼μs) [20,21]. The quenching effect of d-d transitions in the FMOs (mainly the LUMO) compromises PL performance through non-radiative decay pathways, resulting in low emission intensity and short excited state lifetimes. To support this statement, the radiative and non-radiative constants (kr and knr) were analyzed by Equation 1 and Equation 2, with the detailed results presented in Table 1.
(1)
τ = 1 / (
k r
+
k
n r
)
(2)
ϕ =
k r
/
(
k r
+
k
n r
)

As shown in Table 1, the -NH2 group in Ru2 slightly increases its kr to 6.6×105 s-1, compared to that of Ru1 (kr=6.2×105 s-1). Evidently, the electronic density of the emissive state is enhanced by the -NH2 group, leading to an increased kr in Ru2. Meanwhile, the knr of Ru2 is elevated to 65.0×105 s-1 by the -NH2 group, compared to Ru1 (knr=53.1×105 s-1). The effect of the -NH2 group on increasing knr (accelerating non-radiative/thermal relaxation) is more pronounced than its effect on increasing kr (enhancing the electronic density of the emissive state). Consequently, the presence of the -NH2 group in Ru2 leads to a lower emission quantum compared to Ru1, and a shorter lifetime as well. For the CDs, an even shorter lifetime of 13.3 ns is observed, which is consistent with literature values (∼10 ns) [5].

Fig. 4 shows the XRD curves of the as-synthesized CDs, Ru1@CDs, and Ru2@CDs. A broad XRD band peaking around 24o is observed for the as-synthesized CDs which has been assigned as the diffraction of (002) as reported in a literature [6]. The observation of this broad (002) band suggests that these carbon dots consist primarily of amorphous carbon atoms, lacking long-range well-resolved lattice features. Similar (002) diffraction bands are observed for Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs. Despite slight variations in XRD intensity, Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs exhibit nearly identical XRD curves to that of the as-synthesized CDs, with no new peaks or obvious spectral shifts. It can be concluded that the loading of Ru(II) probes (Ru1 or Ru2) onto the CDs has a minimal effect on the crystal lattice of the carbon dots. The morphology of the as-synthesized CDs is revealed through SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 4, round carbon dots with a diameter of approximately 2 nm are uniformly distributed with clear and well-resolved lattice structures. There is no intense aggregation between the carbon dots due to the -NH2 groups on their surface, which prevent particle aggregation through electrostatic repulsion. Upon loading the Ru(II) probe (Ru1 or Ru2), Ru1@CDs remain well-separated, whereas Ru2@CDs shows slight aggregation. A likely explanation is that Ru2 possesses two types of active groups (-NH2 and -COOH), enabling it to act as a bridging component connecting two or even more carbon dots, resulting in the aggregation of Ru2@CDs. This aggregation, however, negatively impacts the luminescence and optical sensing performance, which will be further discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading level and status via IR spectra
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the probe loading level of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, their elemental analysis results are compared with that of the as-synthesized CDs. The C, H, N contents in the CDs are determined as 80.77%, 12.59%, and 1.71%, respectively, while those of Ru1@CDs are measured as 77.12%, 10.89%, and 3.87%, and for Ru2@CDs 75.21%, 10.22%, and 4.80%. With the molecular composition of the two Ru((I) probes in hand, the doping levels (weight%) of the Ru(II) probe are determined to be 18.45% for Ru1@CDs and 26.09% for Ru2@CDs, respectively. These doping levels are significantly lower than the literature values for Ru-doped porous structures, indicating that Ru1 and Ru2 are likely attached onto the surface of the carbon dots [10,17,18]. It is observed that, due to the presence of multiple active groups in Ru2 (-NH2 and -COOH), the probe loading level of Ru2@CDs (weight 26.09%) is significantly higher than that of Ru1@CDs (weight 18.45%). The relationship between the probe loading level and sensing performance will be further discussed.

The bonding status between the Ru(II) probe and the CDs in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs is revealed by comparing the IR spectra of the CDs, Ru1, Ru2, Ru1@CDs, and Ru2@CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , the CDs exhibit a simple IR spectrum with a series of IR bands ranging from 2870 cm-1 to 2970 cm-1 which are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H (originating from ethylenediamine during synthesis) [6]. The sharp IR peak of 1604 cm-1 is assigned to the vibration of the -NH2 group. For Ru1, the IR bands at 3400 cm-1 and 1077 cm-1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of the -COOH group. The IR spectrum of Ru2 is similar to that of Ru1, except for an additional IR peak at 1636 cm-1 which is attributed to the -NH2 group of Ru2. The IR spectrum of Ru1@CDs preserves the IR peaks of CDs (-CH vibration at ∼2965 cm-1 and -NH2 vibration at 1622 cm-1) and Ru1 (-COOH vibration at ∼3349 cm-1). A new IR band appears at 1700 cm-1, which is attributed to the vibration of -CO-NH group. However, the bending vibration of -COOH at 1077 cm-1 is not observed in the IR spectrum of Ru1@CDs. This result suggests that Ru1 has been covalently bonded to the CDs. The situation in Ru2@CDs is similar to that in Ru1@CDs. Nevertheless, the IR intensity of -NH2 (peaking at 1628 cm-1) and -COOH (peaking at 3385 cm-1) remains strong in Ru2@CDs. This indicates that there are still some dangling active bonds in Ru2@CDs (-COOH and -NH2). The presence of multiple active groups in Ru2 appears to negatively impact probe loading. A likely reason is that two or more Ru2 molecules are linearly bonded together via -COOH and -NH2 groups, and the steric hindrance of this structure makes the remaining active groups (-NH2) on the CDs surface inaccessible to Ru2.

The XPS spectra of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs are shown in Figure S5 (Supporting Information). For Ru1@CDs, the C 1s peaks at 284.8 eV and extends to 288.0 eV, corresponding to a mixture of C-C/C=C bonds (284.6 eV) and C-O/C-N bonds (286.0 eV). The N 1s of Ru1@CDs peaks at 399.6 eV with a shoulder peak at 398.8 eV, which are contributed to the N atoms in amino group and aromatic (pyridine) rings. The O 1s of Ru1@CDs peaks at 530.6 eV, with wide distribution around 532.3 eV, corresponding to O=C and O-C bonds. The C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s data of Ru2@CDs are similar to those of Ru1@CDs, peaking at 284.7 eV (C 1s), 399.8 eV and 398.7 eV (N 1s), and 530.6 eV and 532.3 eV (O 1s). The enhanced XPS peak at 532.3 eV of Ru2@CDs, compared to that of Ru1@CDs, is attributed to the higher probe loading level of Ru2@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission, excitation, excited state lifetime, and FRET of Ru1@CD and Ru2@CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As mentioned in Section 3.1.3, the blue emission of the CDs overlaps well with the optimal excitation window of Ru1 and Ru2, suggesting a potential energy transfer from CDs to Ru1/Ru2 through a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The FRET efficiency from donor to acceptor is influenced by multiple factors, as illustrated in Equations 3 and 4, including Q0 (donor intrinsic ϕ), J (spectral overlap integral between donor emission and acceptor absorption/excitation), κ2 (mutual molecular orientation), nd (solvent refraction index), NA (Avogadro's number), λ (wavelength in nm), fd(λ) (donor emission intensity), and εA(λ) (acceptor molar extinction efficient), respectively [22].
(3)
R 0 6
=
9
Q 0
k
2
J
( ln 10 )
128
π
5
n d 4
N A
(4)
J = ∫
f D
( λ )
ε
A
( λ )
λ
4
d λ

The spectral overlap integral J plays a crucial role in enhancing FRET efficiency. Given the good spectral overlap between CDs's emission and Ru1/Ru2’s absorption/excitation, a high FRET efficiency (from CDs to Ru1/Ru2) is anticipated in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs.

To test this hypothesis, the emission and excitation spectra of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs are recorded and discussed. As shown in Fig. 5, Ru1@CDs exhibits a blue emission band peaking at 459 nm and a deep-red emission band peaking at 701 nm. The former is attributed to the CDs, and the latter is assigned to the emission from the probe (Ru1). Compared to the emission bands of intrinsic CDs (λem=453 nm) and Ru1 (λem=710 nm), the blue emission of Ru1@CDs (459 nm) is red-shifted, while the red emission of Ru1@CDs (701 nm) is blue-shifted. The covalent bonding between CDs and Ru1/Ru2 leads to the reorganization of the electronic cloud, resulting in the observed spectral shifts. Similar spectral shifts are observed for Ru2@CDs, showing a blue emission band peaking at 460 nm (compared to 453 nm of intrinsic CDs) and a deep-red emission band peaking at 695 nm (compared to 705 nm of intrinsic Ru2). The relative probe emission intensity of Ru2@CDs (695 nm) is significantly lower than that of Ru1@CDs (701 nm). Given that Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs share the same CDs, and the emission quantum yields of Ru1 and Ru2 are comparable, it is likely that the two types of active groups in Ru2 (-NH2 and -COOH) facilitate the aggregation of Ru2@CDs, leading to self-absorption and self-quenching of Ru2 emission.

The excitation spectra of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs shown in Fig. 5 are compared to those of Ru1 and Ru2 shown in Fig. 3. The excitation spectra of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs consist of two excitation peaks from the CDs (249 nm and 355 nm) and one excitation peak from Ru1 (455 nm) or Ru2 (436 nm). The observation of excitation wavelengths at 249 nm and 355 nm for the Ru emission, which correspond to the excitation of the CDs, suggests that there is an energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru probe (Ru1/Ru2). To further confirm this hypothesis, the excited state lifetimes (τ) of Ru1@CDs are determined to be 9.8 ns at 459 nm and 274.1 ns at 701 nm, while those of Ru2@CDs are determined to be 10.4 ns at 460 nm and 200.3 ns at 695 nm, respectively. Compared to the lifetime values of intrinsic CDs (τ=13.3 ns), Ru1 (τ=168.8 ns), and Ru2 (τ=139.8 ns), the lifetime of the CDs is decreased in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, while those of the Ru(II) are increased. This result confirms the energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe (see Figure S3 and Figure S4 in Supporting Information for the lifetime comparison between CDs, Ru1, Ru2, Ru1@CDs, and Ru2@CDs). The energy transfer efficiency (η) from donor (the CDs) to acceptor (the Ru probe) is calculated by Equation 5. Here τ’ denotes the intrinsic donor lifetime without acceptor and τ means the donor lifetime.
(5)
η = 1 −
τ ′
/ τ
The η values are then calculated as 26.3% for Ru1@CDs and 21.8% for Ru2@CDs, respectively.

Despite the efficient donor-acceptor energy transfer in Ru2@CDs (η=21.8%), the red probe emission from Ru2@CDs is weak, which has been explained above by self-absorption and self-quenching. Since oxygen sensing involves dynamic collisions between O2 molecules and the Ru(II) excited state, using the Ru(II) emission intensity as the sensing output signal, the weak red probe emission from Ru2@CDs makes it unsuitable as an oxygen sensing probe. Thus, only Ru1@CDs is chosen for the oxygen sensing trial.

The sensing performance of Ru1@CDs is discussed by analyzing the emission spectra of Ru1@CDs in solutions with various concentrations of dissolved O2. It is observed from Fig. 6 that at a low concentration of dissolved O2 (0.59 mg/L, denoted as [O2] below), Ru1@CDs emits a blue emission band peaking at 459 nm and a deep-red emission band peaking at 701 nm. A dramatic drop of red emission is observed with O2 existence, which can be explained by two reasons. First, there is a relatively dramatic gap between the first (0.59 mg/L) and the second (3.88 mg/L) concentrations of dissolved oxygen, due to the limitation of our operation to control/adjust the concentration of dissolved O2 (by bubbling N2/O2 mixture into solutions using gas meters). Therefore, the red emission intensity under the second concentration (3.88 mg/L) seemed to be dramatically decreased, compared to that under the first concentration (0.59 mg/L). Second, there might be two kinds of oxygen-sensing sites, one is extremely oxygen-sensitive and the other is relatively insensitive. The amount of the former site is less (or much less) than that of the latter site, leading to the observation of “a dramatic drop with O2 existence”. Similar cases have been reported in literature, including Ru(II)- and Cu(I)-based oxygen sensing systems [23–25]. At an [O2] level of ∼40 mg/L, the deep-red emission is nearly undetectable. This result suggests that the Ru(II) emission is highly sensitive to changes in O2 concentration. However, the intensity of the blue emission is not quenched but slightly increased, due to the following two reasons. First, the excited state lifetime of the CDs is too short (9.8 ns as mentioned above) for the O2 dynamic collision [10,17,18]. Second, the dynamic collision and quenching of O2 suppresses the energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe (Ru1), leading to an increased emission intensity of the CDs as [O2] increases. Although varying [O2] levels affect both emission bands (459 nm and 701 nm), there is no obvious spectral shift or new emission band. This result is consistent with the dynamic quenching mechanism, where a probe (Ru1 in this work) in excited state collides with O2 molecules in ground state (3O2) and transfers its energy to 3O2, as described by Equation 6.
(6)
3
O 2
+ R u
1
*
→
1
O 2
+ R u 1
*means an excited state

To confirm this dynamic oxygen sensing mechanism, the emission lifetimes of Ru1@CDs (701 nm) upon various levels of dissolved oxygen are recorded and shown in Figure S6 (Supporting Information). The lifetime is decreased from 274.1 ns ([O2] = 0.59 mg/L) to 112.4 ns ([O2] = 5.91 mg/L). Here, the dynamic quenching mechanism is confirmed. Then the 1H NMR spectra of Ru1 in D2O with low (bubbled with N2 stream) and high (bubbled with O2 stream) dissolved O2 levels were recorded and are shown in Figure S7 (Supporting Information). The two NMR spectra are nearly identical, with no new peaks or missing peaks. This is because, in a dynamic sensing process, the energy transfer occurs through a dynamic collision between the excited state probe and O2, having no tight relation with the probe ground state. A minor shift (δ 8.62 under low O2 level to δ 8.64 under high O2 level) is observed, which may be caused by the interference between O2 and the -COOH group of Ru1.The relative intensity variation of the deep-red emission band from Ru1 upon various [O2] against the well-preserved blue emission intensity from the CDs causes a color change from deep-red at low [O2] levels to blue at high [O2] levels, as shown in Fig. 6.

The oxygen sensing response of Ru1@CDs to various [O2] levels is analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation [10,17,18]. When a probe is uniformly distributed in a homogeneous microenvironment and it follows a dynamic quenching mechanism, the intensity form of the Stern-Volmer equation can be described by Equation 7, where I denotes the emission intensity (I0 represents the initial intensity without any quencher), KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [O2] is the oxygen concentration, respectively.
(7)
I 0
/
I
= 1 +
K
S V
·
[
O 2
]

From the equation, it is assumed that the calibration will be linear with a slope of KSV . The
I 0
/
I
plots generally follow a linear correlation with [O2], as shown in Fig. 6, with the fitting equation of
I 0
/
I
= 1.462 + 3.716 · [
O 2
]
, R2=0.996. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.12 mg/L (3σ/N).

Considering the generally-preserved blue emission band at 459 nm, its emission intensity (I459) is tentatively used as a reference, and the Stern-Volmer equation can be revised to Equation 8, and fitted to
I 459
/
I 701
= − 2.022 + 1.289 · [
O 2
]
, R2=0.996. The LOD was determined to be 3.03 mg/L (3σ/N). These parameters are worse than those of the fitting equation of
I 0
/
I
plot. On the other hand, the
I 459
/
I 701
plot will find its advantage when considering selectivity, which will be discussed below.
(8)
I 459
/
I 701
= 1 +
K
S V
·
[
O 2
]

An optical sensing platform is based on the optical signal variation in the presence of analyte, thus, a unique sensing response towards a specific analyte is highly desired, which ensures precise results. The sensing selectivity of Ru1@CDs is discussed by analyzing its emission spectra in the presence of common competing species and interferents, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, H+, H2O2, and glutathione (GSH). As shown in Fig. 6, metal cations with a closed-shell structure, such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and GSH, generally have a slight quenching effect on the Ru(II) emission and the CDs emission with
I 459
/
I 701
values of 0.198-0.232 (vs 0.215 for blank sample). As for metal cations with an open-shell structure, such as Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+, the quenching effect is more pronounced, showing
I 459
/
I 701
values of 0.151-0.181. This is because their open-shell structure is an energy-harvesting system which quenches the energy of both the CDs and the Ru(II) probe. H2O2 shows the strongest interfering effect with an
I 459
/
I 701
value of 0.610 since it releases active O2 which quenches the excited state of the Ru(II) probe as dissolved O2 does. These
I 459
/
I 701
values in the presence of interferents, however, are much lower than that in the presence of O2 (52.463), as shown in Fig. 6. Thus, result precision can be guaranteed.

Upon various pH values (3.9-8.5), the emission spectra and corresponding
I 459
/
I 701
values of Ru1@CDs were recorded and shown in Figure S8 (Supporting Information). It appears that an acidic solution tends to enhance the emission intensity of the red emission (701 nm). The highest
I 459
/
I 701
value is observed at pH=6.5. Either a higher or a lower pH value tends to decrease the
I 459
/
I 701
value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal stability, response time and practical performance
ce:section.ce:para:
As stated previously, an optical sensing platform is based on the optical signal variation in the presence of analyte, thus, signal stability is another important factor to be considered. The red emission intensity (701 nm) is continuously monitored as [O2] increases. It is observed from Fig. 6 that the emission intensity is well preserved when [O2] is low, suggesting good signal stability (within 0-60 s). The storage- and photo-stability of Ru1@CDs are recorded and shown in Figure S9, Figure S10, and Figure S11 (Supporting Information). A minor blueshift of the Ru(II) emission (701 nm) is observed after 14 aging days, which may be attributed to the oxidization effect. Good photo-stability is observed within 0-1000 seconds. We attribute the excellent signal stability to the high photostability of the Ru(II) probe. In addition, the indirect excitation mechanism (energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe) may enhance the photostability of the Ru(II) probe. After increasing [O2], the red emission intensity rapidly decreases within 60-100 s and then stabilizes (within 100-200 s) and eventually becomes constant (longer than 200 s). The entire sensing response is completed within 200 s, and this response time (200-60=140 s) is slightly longer than that for gaseous state sensing (<60 s) [10,17,18]. A possible reason is that the dynamic collision of the Ru(II) probe with dissolved [O2] is less efficient than the dynamic collision with gaseous O2, leading to a longer response time. A performance comparison between this work and literature reports is demonstrated in Table S3 (Supporting Information), which confirmed the oxygen sensing behavior of Ru1@CDs, including the improved sensitivity and linear calibration curve. On the other hand, the response time needs to be decreased. Next, the practical sensing performance of Ru1@CDs is evaluated, and the result of sensing accuracy is shown in Table 2 . It is observed that Ru1@CDs tends to give positive errors. A possible reason is that there are competing species in real samples, such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ that are able to quench the Ru(II) emission and thus exhibit fake signals, leading to positive errors. In the [O2] range of 3-40 mg/L, the
I 0
/
I
vs [O2] calibration curve shows lower sensing errors than
I 459
/
I 701
vs [O2], even though the latter curve uses self-calibrated ratios. A possible reason is that the intensity at 459 nm is slightly increased as [O2] levels increase, thereby decreasing the Stern-Volmer constant and thus leads to higher errors.

In conclusion, we synthesized two Ru(II) probes with terpyridine-derived ligands containing different bonding groups (-NH2 and -COOH) to compare their sensing performance. The two Ru(II) probes were discussed in detail, covering their molecular structure, absorption, emission, excitation, quantum yields, and lifetime. These Ru(II) probes were covalently bonded with the blue-emitting carbon dots prepared by a classic hydrothermal method using the fibers of Euonymus bungeanus as a starting material, using the blue emission as the excitation source. The resulting composite sample was analyzed by micromorphological analysis, XRD, IR, and elemental analysis. Ru1@CDs showed good sensing selectivity and a linear correlation with the concentration of dissolved oxygen, with a fitting equation of
I 0
/
I
= 1.462 + 3.716 · [
O 2
]
, a LOD of 0.12 mg/L, and a short response time of ∼140 s. It was found that the excess probe loading onto the CDs led to self-absorption and self-quenching. The presence of transition metal cations, H+, and H2O2 exerted significant interference on sensing signals. In future work, both the sensing selectivity and the Stern-Volmer constant should be improved. The energy transfer efficiency from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe also needs to be enhanced. In addition, the sensing-related parameters of the Ru(II) probe, such as emission quantum and lifetime, should also be improved.
Supporting Information

Below information is listed in the Supporting Information, including: an explanation on the selection of raw materials and why the CDs with many kinds of surface groups could be acted as reference; Tables S1, S2, and S3; Figures S1-S11, 1H NMR spectra and geometric parameters of Ru1 and Ru2.
Xiaojuan Zhao: Investigation. Yun Ye: Writing – original draft. Xiuya Ye: Formal analysis. Ruimin Li: Resources. Yongqiang Liao: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the fanatical support from the following funds, including Guangdong Provincial Key R&D Program in Priority Areas (2023B0202100001); Key Scientific Research Platforms and Projects of Colleges and Universities by the Guangdong Provincial Department of Education (2024ZDZX4134); Guangdong Province Science and Technology Department Project (KTP20240745).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2025.142349.
